meta.note: Coach moments are inserted mid-session. One per unit where assigned. Character: marco = calm mentor, old-chen = wise sensei.

coach_moments.title: A frame is not a push — it's a structure.
coach_moments.body: When you push, you give up position the moment they push back. When you frame — elbow locked, bones stacked — you create a wall. Their strength goes into the structure, not into moving you. Frame first, escape second.

coach_moments.title: The shrimp is your most important move on the ground.
coach_moments.body: If you only learned one thing in BJJ, it would be the shrimp. Every escape, every guard recovery, every position change — all of them have shrimp inside them. When you don't know what to do on the bottom: shrimp.

coach_moments.title: Bridge with your whole body, not your neck.
coach_moments.body: Beginners bridge with their neck and shoulders. The Upa is a FULL BODY movement — hips drive to the ceiling, your body rotates. The hips have the power. The neck is just along for the ride.

coach_moments.title: The paintbrush is a technique inside a technique.
coach_moments.body: Before any arm lock from top, you must own the arm. The paintbrush slides it flat — like painting the floor with their forearm. Without this, you're fighting their strength. With it, their arm is already lost.

coach_moments.title: Row with your back — not your arms.
coach_moments.body: The Rear Naked Choke fails for most beginners because they squeeze with their arms. The finishing motion is a ROW — elbows toward each other, back muscles engaging. Biceps are weak. Your back is strong.

coach_moments.title: Break posture first. Always.
coach_moments.body: Every guard attack has the same prerequisite: their posture is broken. Collar choke, triangle, armbar — none of them work against a man sitting tall. Break posture first. Then your attacks become effortless.

coach_moments.title: Every grip has a weak side — and it's always the thumb.
coach_moments.body: When someone grabs you, their fingers form a ring. The weakest point is where the thumb closes. Pull toward the thumb gap, and any grip breaks. This works on wrists, collars, lapels — everything.

coach_moments.title: Posture in guard is a position, not just a feeling.
coach_moments.body: Posture means: spine straight, hips low and pressing, one arm posted, chin up. Not just 'trying to sit up.' Each of those four things matters. Lose one and the attacks start working.

coach_moments.title: Strength makes you slower and easier to sweep.
coach_moments.body: When a white belt tenses up and uses all their muscle, two things happen: they gas out in minutes, and their body becomes rigid — rigid bodies are easier to move. Relax. Use your technique. A relaxed body feels the movement and responds. A tense body just breaks.

coach_moments.title: Don't be a YouTube warrior.
coach_moments.body: There's a temptation to copy what you see online — berimbolos, heel hooks, spinning submissions. Resist it. Those moves are built on top of the fundamentals. Without a solid shrimp, Upa, and proper posture — advanced techniques are a house built on sand. Master the boring stuff first.

coach_moments.title: In BJJ, failing is just learning with extra steps.
coach_moments.body: You will tap. You will get swept. You will make every mistake in this app and many more on the mat. That's the deal. Every tap is information — your body is learning what doesn't work. Don't fight the tap. Embrace it.

coach_moments.title: Flow rolls: the secret weapon of fast learners.
coach_moments.body: A flow roll is when you and your partner explore positions together — no ego, no survival mode. One person takes top, gives a sweep, the other comes to mount, offers an escape. You learn more in 5 minutes of flowing than 30 minutes of grinding. This app simulates that — each question is a position to explore, not a fight.
